summary: Gruber rounds up early iPhone X reviews after having only 24 hours with the device himself. He shares quick impressions — Face ID works great, the notch is a non-issue, but the missing home button takes adjustment. He highlights reviews from Panzarino, Nguyen, and Ulanoff, with particular attention to Dan Riccio's on-record denial that Touch ID was ever planned for iPhone X, the OLED display's off-axis blue shift, and the puzzling dead space below the keyboard. He notes the single-face limitation of Face ID and questions Apple's claim that few people register multiple fingerprints.
key_points: Dan Riccio flatly denied reports that Apple scrambled to replace Touch ID, saying they committed fully to Face ID once they had early confidence it would work
The iPhone X OLED display is impressive but shows a noticeable blue shift and saturation drop at off-axis viewing angles
The dead space below the keyboard exists deliberately to prevent accidental home gesture activation while typing
Face ID's single-face registration is a real limitation compared to Touch ID's support for multiple fingerprints, which parents and spouses actually use
Multiple reviewers agree that Face ID makes the phone feel like it has no passcode at all
money_quotes: Face ID works great. In practice it's like not even having a passcode on the phone. You just swipe and you're in.
I don't really notice the notch while using it.
I do notice the lack of a home button. I think I'll get used to the new no-home-button UI soon, but 10 years of habits die hard.
I hate to say it, but it makes the iPhone 8 Plus LCD look kind of like butt.

For a normal human who isn't aware of the 30,000 invisible dots being projected on their face or the 3D map of their head encrypted somewhere deep inside their phone, there's nothing 'futuristic' about these interactions.
Using Face ID is what life without a passcode — life before we all became paranoid technofreaks — felt like.
There's also the simple and obvious fact that humans have 10 fingers, but just one face.
Last week my wife let me put a fingerprint on her iPhone so I could use Apple Pay while pre-ordering her iPhone X while she slept.
key_insight: The iPhone X's biggest innovations — Face ID and the edge-to-edge display — succeed precisely because they disappear in practice, making the phone feel simpler rather than more complex.
